serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Kossa is approximately 200g, equivalent to a medium restaurant portion served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Kossa — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Kossa (G00010793), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 50% of total fat (5.79g/100g) based on USDA FoodData Central mutton composition data (USDA SR Legacy #17174) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish; these fields are set to -1 and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Kossa. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry with low carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g), consistent with GI values for meat-based dishes with spiced gravy reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate contribution is primarily from onion, tomato, and spice paste. GL per 200g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g with low potassium (210mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.3g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Mutton Kossa to portions of 100–120g, request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to partially offset the sodium load of 467mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44g/100g) and high protein (14.05g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but high fat (11.58g/100g) may cause delayed postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Kossa is acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes patients in moderate portions (150–180g) due to its low carbohydrate content and high protein, but should not be paired with refined carbohydrates such as white rice or naan in large quantities, and post-meal glucose monitoring is advised given the high fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.79g/100g, cholesterol 85mg/100g, and sodium 467mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Kossa sparingly, limiting portions to 100g or less, as the combination of saturated fat (~5.8g/100g), cholesterol (85mg/100g), and high sodium (467mg/100g) contributes to LDL elevation, arterial stiffness, and blood pressure burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat ~5.79g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; tomato and chilli are nightshade derivatives
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton Kossa is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its high arachidonic acid content from red meat, elevated saturated fat, and the presence of nightshade ingredients (tomato, red chilli powder) which may exacerbate joint inflammation in nightshade-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Kossa (also known as Kosha Mangsho or Mutton Kosha) is a slow-cooked Bengali-style mutton curry popular in Indian communities in Singapore and across South Asia. The dish is prepared by braising bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in a rich, deeply spiced gravy made from caramelised onions, tomatoes, ginger-garlic paste, red chilli powder, turmeric, garam masala, and mustard oil, with yogurt added to tenderise the meat and enrich the sauce. The cooking process involves extended slow-cooking or pressure-cooking to break down the tough connective tissue of mutton, resulting in tender, fall-off-the-bone meat coated in a thick, intensely flavoured dark gravy. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and slightly tangy from the tomato and yogurt, with a deep umami richness from the caramelised onion base. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat (approximately 5.8g saturated), 7.44g carbohydrate, 4.69g sugar, 0.3g dietary fibre, and 467mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic stability, making it moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients in controlled portions. However, the high sodium (467mg/100g) and saturated fat (~5.8g/100g) make it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the arachidonic acid content of red meat and the presence of nightshade ingredients. Mutton Kossa is typically served with steamed rice, roti, or paratha and is a common feature at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is Mutton Kossa good for diabetes?
Can I eat Mutton Kossa if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010793) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy mutton composition as HPB does not separately report this field.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific Mutton Kossa preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010793) entry, and USDA FoodData Central does not provide an equivalent composite Bengali-style mutton curry entry with fatty acid breakdown as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg set to -1: the full vitamin panel for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010793), and no equivalent composite dish entry with complete vitamin data exists in USDA FoodData Central for Bengali-style mutton curry as of 2026. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010793) for this dish, and no equivalent composite entry in USDA FoodData Central provides these values for a Bengali-style mutton curry preparation as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010793) as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 5.79g/100g (approximately 50% of total fat) based on USDA SR Legacy #17174 (lamb/mutton, cooked) as a defensible proxy; documented in nutrient_source. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database meat-based curry analogues; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Kossa exists in the published literature. Halal status assumed based on standard mutton preparation without pork-derived ingredients; kosher status not confirmed. The description notes 'Bengali style' which aligns with Kosha Mangsho preparation methodology.
